commands.repair_reason: There is no narrow automated repair blocker left, but the protected maintainer label and retention-policy tradeoff require maintainer review.; Cleared: The diff touches gateway filesystem retention logic and tests only; it adds no dependency, workflow, permission, credential, network, or command-execution surface. (sha=00d0fde63093fb7f4b3e4c6d9bdcbf4a73b7b8b4)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a fixed 128 MiB per-session retained-byte budget for compaction checkpoint snapshots while preserving the existing count cap and adding filesystem-backed regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +64, Tests +93. Total +157 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection and the provided live evidence: current main retains up to 25 checkpoint snapshots by count only, while the linked report shows many 14-16 MB checkpoint files for one session. I did not run a live gateway repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: There is no narrow automated repair blocker left, but the protected maintainer label and retention-policy tradeoff require maintainer review.

target.title: fix(gateway): cap retained compaction checkpoint bytes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: compaction checkpoint retention was count-only, so repeated compactions could retain many large full-transcript checkpoint snapshot files for one session.
- Solution: keep the existing count cap and add a fixed 128 MiB retained checkpoint snapshot byte budget per session, trimming older checkpoint metadata and files when either limit is exceeded.
- What changed: `persistSessionCompactionCheckpoint` now builds the retained checkpoint set inside the session-store writer, stats those writer-owned snapshot paths before trimming, and applies both count and byte limits; regression coverage uses real filesystem sparse checkpoint files.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no checkpoint file format changes, no protocol changes, no new config surface, and no broad gateway scheduler/backpressure changes.

## Motivation

- The live performance investigation found many retained `.checkpoint.<uuid>.jsonl` artifacts around 14-16 MB each in a single active agent session directory. Count-only retention allowed this to grow far beyond the amount of checkpoint data needed for recovery.
- This is an on-disk artifact retention fix. It reduces retained checkpoint files and page-cache/read-I/O pressure; it does not change model context size or heap memory limits.
- A fixed internal 128 MiB cap keeps roughly eight observed-size checkpoints while preventing the previous worst case of 25 retained snapshots up to 64 MiB each.
- The cap is intentionally not configurable in this PR. If real deployments later need a different retention policy, a separate config surface can be added with docs/schema ownership at that point.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84822
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: repeated compaction checkpoints now retain by total snapshot bytes per session instead of count only.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw worktree on Ubuntu/WSL2 with Node 24.15.0; regression uses real temp session-store JSON and real sparse checkpoint files on disk.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.ts src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- Evidence after fix: terminal capture from the focused test run:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /home/galini/GitHub/worktrees/bug-026-033-gateway-perf

Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  24 passed (24)
  Start at  22:34:04
  Duration  10.88s (transform 4.17s, setup 629ms, import 9.40s, tests 326ms, environment 0ms)
```

- Observed result after fix: the new test creates eight old checkpoint files plus a current checkpoint file sized to exceed the 128 MiB retained-byte budget, persists a checkpoint, verifies the three oldest checkpoint files are deleted, verifies the newest old checkpoint files and current checkpoint remain, and verifies stored metadata keeps only the retained checkpoints.
- What was not tested: a live long-running gateway compaction cycle was not rerun against a private production-like agent session; the proof is a filesystem-backed gateway checkpoint seam test plus redacted before evidence from the live sample.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): redacted live sample showed many retained checkpoint files around 14-16 MB each, with earlier same-investigation `pidstat` read I/O averaging `kB_rd/s=40768.06` over a 5s sample.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: compaction checkpoint retention used only `MAX_COMPACTION_CHECKPOINTS_PER_SESSION = 25`, while each retained snapshot could be up to 64 MiB.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered count trimming but not retained snapshot bytes.
- Contributing context (if known): memory-host session scanning already skips checkpoint artifacts, so the remaining pressure was the retained checkpoint artifact set itself.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/session-compaction-checkpoints.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: persisting a checkpoint trims older checkpoint metadata and checkpoint files when retained snapshot bytes exceed the fixed per-session budget.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: checkpoint persistence owns the metadata and file cleanup decision, and the test uses real files without requiring a live gateway session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing count-trim coverage only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Long-running sessions retain fewer old compaction checkpoint snapshots on disk when their checkpoint artifacts exceed 128 MiB per session. This is an internal retention policy, not a new operator-facing config knob.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[repeated compactions] -> [up to 25 full checkpoint snapshots retained]

After:
[repeated compactions] -> [newest checkpoints retained within count + 128 MiB budget] -> [older checkpoint files removed]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu/WSL2
- Runtime/container: Node 24.15.0, local worktree
- Model/provider: N/A for checkpoint retention
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): temp session store in the regression test

### Steps

1. Create a session store with eight retained checkpoint metadata entries pointing at real checkpoint files.
2. Size each checkpoint file so the existing checkpoints plus the new checkpoint exceed the fixed per-session retained-byte budget.
3. Persist a new compaction checkpoint.

### Expected

Older checkpoint metadata and files are trimmed until retained checkpoint bytes fit the 128 MiB budget while the newest checkpoint remains available.

### Actual

After this patch, the regression test verifies the three oldest checkpoint files are deleted and only the newest six checkpoint entries remain.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: byte-budget checkpoint trimming with real temp files; existing checkpoint capture/fork/persist coverage still passes.
- Edge cases checked: current checkpoint remains retained even when byte trimming removes older checkpoints; count-trim behavior still passes; checkpoint size stats use the writer-owned store entry and no pre-writer session-store reread remains; lint no longer flags mutating `reverse()` calls.
- What you did **not** verify: live private-session repeated compaction under gateway load.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: older checkpoints become unavailable sooner for very large sessions.
  - Mitigation: the newest checkpoint is always retained, the existing count cap remains, and the 128 MiB budget still allows multiple 14-16 MB checkpoints from the observed sample.
- Risk: some operators might eventually want a different retention budget.
  - Mitigation: avoid adding public config before there is demonstrated operator need; a future PR can add a config surface with schema/docs if real deployments need it.
